elements.sent-2411-001: The autobidding system generates huge revenue for advertising platforms, garnering substantial research attention.
elements.sent-2411-002: Existing studies in autobidding systems focus on designing Autobidding Incentive Compatible (AIC) mechanisms, where the mechanism is Incentive Compatible (IC) under ex ante expectations.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, upon deploying AIC mechanisms in advertising platforms, we observe a notable deviation between the actual auction outcomes and these expectations during runtime, particularly in the scene with few clicks (sparse-click).
elements.sent-2411-004: This discrepancy undermines truthful bidding among advertisers in AIC mechanisms, especially for risk-averse advertisers who are averse to outcomes that do not align with the expectations.
elements.sent-2411-005: To address this issue, we propose a mechanism, Decoupled First-Price Auction (DFP), that retains its IC property even during runtime.
elements.sent-2411-006: DFP dynamically adjusts the payment based on real-time user conversion outcomes, ensuring that advertisers' realized utilities closely approximate their expected utilities during runtime.
elements.sent-2411-007: To realize the payment mechanism of DFP, we propose a PPO-based RL algorithm, with a meticulously crafted reward function.
elements.sent-2411-008: This algorithm dynamically adjusts the payment to fit DFP mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-009: We conduct extensive experiments leveraging real-world data to validate our findings.
elements.sent-2411-010: According to statistics from the Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB), online advertising has generated hundreds of billions of dollars in annual revenue for internet companies.
elements.sent-2411-011: In the traditional real-time bidding (RTB), each user query triggers an auction within the platform, where the auctioned items are the advertisement (ad) slots positioned from top to bottom on the search results page view (PV).
elements.sent-2411-012: Bidders in these auctions are advertisers interested in the specific query.
elements.sent-2411-013: During each auction, bidders independently submit their bids to the platform, considering various factors such as global constraints (e.g., budget, return on investment (ROI), or target cost per acquisition (tCPA)) and value per ad (e.g., per-click or per-conversion value).
elements.sent-2411-014: The platform then operates an auction mechanism to determine the allocation of slots and the payments made by the bidders based on their bids.
elements.sent-2411-015: Based on auction theory \cite{vickrey1961counterspeculation, clarke1971multipart, groves1973incentives, myerson1981optimal , the earliest mechanisms in RTB were the Generalized First Price (GFP) \cite{cary2007greedy, lahaie2007sponsored, edelman2007strategic, jansen2008sponsored and Generalized Second Price (GSP) \cite{edelman2007internet auctions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: With advancements in machine learning and the increasing availability of real-time data, an autobidding technology has gained growing attention and has been adopted in ad auctions \cite{aggarwal2019autobidding, babaioff2020non, deng2021towards, balseiro2021landscape, mehta2022auction .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In autobidding, bidders are value-maximizers, aiming to maximize the total value acquired over multiple rounds of auctions (rounds) under certain constraints.
elements.sent-2411-018: Bidders submit their constraints and values to the platform.
elements.sent-2411-019: The platform configures an autobidder for each bidder to bid on their behalf in each round.
elements.sent-2411-020: Furthermore, the pricing model in the auction dictates when and how bidders pay.
elements.sent-2411-021: The earliest pricing model included cost-per-click (CPC) and cost-per-acquisition (CPA), where advertisers bid/pay for clicks and conversions (acquisitions), respectively.
elements.sent-2411-022: Recently, optimized-cost-per-click (OCPC), a model tailored to bidders' ultimate conversion goals, has emerged.
elements.sent-2411-023: In OCPC, advertisers bid for conversions, while the platform charges them upon each click.
elements.sent-2411-024: OCPC encapsulates the merits of both CPC and CPA, offering a mutually beneficial framework for both advertisers and platforms.
elements.sent-2411-025: By empowering advertisers to bid based on conversions, OCPC incentivizes platforms to deliver more precisely targeted advertisements, thereby enhancing conversion rates.
elements.sent-2411-026: Meanwhile, the click-based payment mechanism ensures that advertisers maintain greater control over their costs, while the platform secures a stable revenue stream.
elements.sent-2411-027: Different from previous work which mainly focuses on CPC or CPA models, our work focuses on tCPA bidders in OCPC model.
elements.sent-2411-028: Most research on autobidding has been focused on the ex ante properties of auctions, emphasizing bidders' utilities and constraints under expectation, with the objective of designing mechanisms that satisfy both Autobidding Incentive Compatibility (AIC) and Individual Rationality (IR) \cite{alimohammadi2023incentive, xing2023truthful .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Inspired by those related papers, we design an AIC mechanism, Coupled First-Price Auction (CFP), where the allocation mechanism accommodates the broadest spectrum of ranking scores, thereby ensuring flexibility and adaptability to cater to any allocation objectives of platforms.
elements.sent-2411-030: However, upon deploying CFP, we observed that bidders, particularly those sparse-click bidders, frequently resubmit bids, often with significant adjustments every day.
elements.sent-2411-031: Analyzing authentic data from our platform (exemplified in Figure \ref{demo for a bidder, where the horizontal axis represents the change in click volume, and the vertical axis represents the conversion volume), we find that the bidder's actual conversion volumes closely align with expectations only when click volumes are substantial (after 1400 clicks).}
elements.sent-2411-032: Conversely, in most instances (before 1400 clicks), notable deviations between actual and expected conversions are observed.
elements.sent-2411-033: This divergence from expected conversions causes the utilities of these bidders in the AIC mechanism to deviate from its expectations.
elements.sent-2411-034: For instance, as illustrated in Figure \ref{demo , when the click volume reaches 800, the bidder observes that the actual conversion volume falls significantly short of her expectation.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Consequently, her realized utility is less than anticipated, and the actual CPA surpasses her true tCPA.
elements.sent-2411-036: This discrepancy incentivizes her to submit a bid to the platform that is lower than her true tCPA.
elements.sent-2411-037: Prior studies implicitly assumed that bidders are risk-neutral (i.e., they only care about the expected outcomes without considering the variations during runtime).
elements.sent-2411-038: Specifically, they assume that bidders have no strategic behaviors that arise from the discrepancies between actual and expected outcomes across multiple auction rounds.
elements.sent-2411-039: However, our observation of bidders frequently resubmitting revised bids indicates that a number of bidders are risk-averse (the most common risk preference type).
elements.sent-2411-040: A risk-averse bidder hopes her real-time utility to match the expectations.
elements.sent-2411-041: An outcome that does not meet expectations prompt risk-averse bidders to adjust their bids or even withdraw from the auction, leading to untruthful bidding and contributing to the instability of the AIC mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-042: We prove that risk-averse bidders will bid truthfully in an AIC mechanism only when the click volume is at least $\frac{(2+ \epsilon) \ln{\frac{1 {\epsilon {\epsilon^2 \cdot \eta}$ for each bidder, where $\epsilon$ is a small positive constant, and $\eta$ denotes the maximum conversion rate (Lemma \ref{lemma5 ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: This requirement poses a challenge for both small and medium-sized bidders (clicks are sparse) and platforms.
elements.sent-2411-044: Put simply, the AIC mechanism works primarily for systems with sufficient click volumes, where real-time outcomes converge towards expectations.
elements.sent-2411-045: To address the challenge of sparse clicks, we introduce a stricter notion of IC than AIC, named Time-Invariant Incentive Compatibility (TIC), in which risk-averse bidders bid truthfully.
elements.sent-2411-046: We characterize the conditions for a mechanism to satisfy TIC (Lemma \ref{lemma6 ), and desgin a TIC mechanism, Decoupled First-Price Auction (DFP, see Figure \ref{Decoupled Auction ).}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Traditional mechanisms have a direct mapping from bids to per-click payments, such as GFP sets the payments equal to corresponding bids and GSP sets the payments equal to the next highest bid.
elements.sent-2411-048: Instead, in DFP, the bids in each round are not explicitly mapped to the per-click payments.
elements.sent-2411-049: Namely, the allocation and payment mechanisms operate independently.
elements.sent-2411-050: In this paper, we demonstrate the theoretical feasibility of decoupling the intricate problem of mechanism design into two distinct, yet interdependent, online optimization problems, aiming to design allocation mechanisms and payment mechanisms, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-051: The allocation mechanism is to maximize certain objectives of the platform.
elements.sent-2411-052: We insert real-time outcomes from users and bidders (even delayed feedback is allowed) between the allocation mechanism and the payment mechanism, and adjust the per-click payments in the payment mechanism accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-053: This approach allows our mechanism not only rely on expectations, but also incorporate users' real-time outcomes into the payment mechanism to satisfy TIC (Theorem \ref{theorem2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-054: The allocation mechanism of DFP is a canonical online optimization problem, which can be addressed utilizing various algorithms such as online algorithms, deep learning, and reinforcement learning (RL), among others \cite{mehta2007adwords, devanur2009adwords .}
elements.sent-2411-055: As we can leverage any allocation mechanism in DFP, it is not the focus of this paper and we focus on the payment mechanism design.
elements.sent-2411-056: Distinct from the majority of online decision-making problems that solely necessitate pricing decisions, the payment mechanism of DFP necessitates two task: firstly, leveraging historical data to forecast the actual conversion outcomes of future users, and secondly, adjusting the per-click payment accordingly based on these forecasts.
elements.sent-2411-057: To realize this intricate payment mechanism in DFP, we propose a PPO-based RL algorithm, with meticulously crafted reward function.
elements.sent-2411-058: Our contributions can be summarized as follows: Propose an AIC Mechanism.

elements.sent-2411-062: We find bidders untruthful bidding because of the deviations of actual outcomes from their expectations in AIC mechanisms, particularly in sparse-click systems.

elements.sent-2411-064: We introduce a new concept, termed Time-Invariant Incentive Compatibility (TIC), which ensures that risk-averse bidders will engage in truthful bidding.
elements.sent-2411-065: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to consider bidders' risk preferences and the real-time bidding processes in autobidding scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-066: Design a TIC Mechanism by Decoupling.

elements.sent-2411-071: We employ a PPO-based RL to construct the payment mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-072: Through meticulous design of the reward function, our payment mechanism dynamically adjusts per-click payments based on historical bidding outcomes and the anticipating of future user conversions.. Conduct Extensive Experiments.
